chapters.title: Relentless entrepreneurs driven by spirit of change
chapters.text:
It's amazing how everyone wants to be an entrepreneur these days. But if we were to go back in time to just a few decades ago, you'll see that not many people wanted to venture into the risky world of entrepreneurship. There was job security, and most people worked in one career all their active years, so there was no motivation to take the risk.

Things have changed incredibly: multiple economic depressions and a global recession have forced millions out of their stable jobs. As such, the desire for freedom and control over the future has made entrepreneurship something to be desired. Everyone and their grandma want to own a business these days.

The rise of global change-makers like Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, and the like is also a contributing factor. The way these popular entrepreneurs are worshiped in the media is making people desire the kinds of feats they've achieved.

Is entrepreneurship really something to be desired? Absolutely. And of course, this summary will teach you how to become a successful one. But before we go into all that, you must know something, and that will determine whether this summary is worth your time or not. The thing is, entrepreneurship is not for everyone.

As glorious as creating businesses and selling them may be, that kind of stuff isn't for the entire population. Millions have failed in business but succeeded as enhancers, helping others grow their business. This goes to say some people will only find success and fulfillment working for others.

You should know what side of the fence your unique wiring falls before leaping to start your first business. Are you ready to pay the price that entrepreneurship demands?

The journey isn't as cozy as the media depicts. When you look past the glory, you'll see that entrepreneurs live mostly lonely lives. Confusion and uncertainties are also things they deal with consistently.

All successful entrepreneurs are risk-takers who refuse to settle for less until they achieve their lofty dreams.

Ask yourself if these are things you're willing to endure. If you're certain about giving it a shot, then let's dive into the proven strategies for building a seven-figure business from the ground up.

chapters.title: Audience research paves way for product selection
chapters.text:
Aspiring entrepreneurs new to the startup process often get stuck deciding what products to sell. It can be especially tricky if you've never started a business before. However, it's important not to make the mistake of spending too much time at this stage. In this chapter, you'll learn how to select the right products that people are willing to pay for.

First things first, don’t start selling randomly just because you've identified products that sell. That's what newbies do. They search the Internet for hot products and then get them at wholesale prices to sell. That's a short-term strategy. A competitor can knock you off any time, particularly because you're just selling on merchant sites and don't have real customers.

Successful businesses are built through branding, not quick sales on merchant sites you don't have control over.

What to do instead? Build a brand that sells niche products to clearly defined customers. That way, even if Amazon shuts down today, your customers will still know how to find you. A brand is beyond just logos, a fancy website, good packaging, and all. Those things are important, but they aren't what makes a brand. For your business to be called a brand, it has to serve a particular market segment with products that are dear to that market.

For example, selling female clothes randomly on Amazon is a good business, and it can be profitable. But if you want to build a lasting brand, you have to take it up a notch by clearly defining and niching down on a particular target market, say, breastfeeding moms. The sort of clothes women wear when breastfeeding is different from what single women and empty nesters wear. Deciding on a particular niche also means you can deeply study the market and know the range of products that will serve them.

You can’t really be happy unless you’re doing something that benefits other people. ~ Ryan Daniel Moran

Ideally, you should have three to five products serving the same audience. It could be more, but you can also start with just one product and develop others as you grow.

How to find a product to sell:

• “Scratch your own itch” is a startup term that simply means identifying a problem you have, creating a solution for it, and selling that solution to others. But it mustn't be your own problem; it should be one the people around you have.
• Identifying the problem will give you ideas on the sort of product you can create to solve it. This product could be something novel or an improvement of what already exists.

Did you know? Color is said to increase brand recognition by up to 80%.

chapters.title: Creating successful products is easy
chapters.text:
You've found an ideal market you want to serve and have an idea of the kind of product they need. What's needed now is to create the product; how do you go about it?

It's quite easy. All you have to do is find private label companies in your region or contact contract manufacturers on Alibaba.com. There are other online platforms for connecting with contract manufacturers. You could Google them, but you probably won't need that because numerous Chinese manufacturing companies are waiting on Alibaba to take your orders.

Once you find a suitable manufacturer, the next step is sharing your ideas with them. Be as clear about it as possible. You'll discuss prices and the turnaround time you should expect for the products. Ideally, you should test the product and ensure it meets your requirements before asking for multiple supplies.

Contract manufacturing saves costs and helps you make maximum profits.

In this testing stage, you can choose to experiment with yourself or a small group of people that represent your target audience.

Copy the tactics software companies use. Give out the product for free to these small groups of people and get their reviews, just as software companies do. It's important to note your product development stages because you'll need it when marketing. Few things are convincing, like showing how your products are different from those of your competitors. Also, being open about your development stages is a good medium for proving that.

chapters.title: Social media provides powerful leverage for business growth
chapters.text:
Do you know how media personality and businesswoman Kylie Jenner became the youngest billionaire in the world? By maximizing her media influence.

When she launched Kylie Cosmetics, she already had a huge online and offline following. It was easy to convert those die-hard followers into paying customers. This sort of media power is what social media influencers and course creators also leverage to drive massive sales.

Not everyone is a Kylie Jenner, but with or without media influence, any entrepreneur can use social media as a sales tool. Let's discuss how you can do this in your pre-launch phase, using a case study from one of Ryan Moran's businesses.

It’s hard to find things that won’t sell online. ~ Jeff Bezos

Ryan Moran and his partner wanted to start selling yoga products. They ordered the products, but it was going to take eight weeks before they got to them. They thought of a way to create awareness before launch, and the idea for a Facebook page struck Moran.

They created the page, called it “I Love Yoga,” and began creating content. Slowly, it started to gain traction. People were following and engaging with the page. Moran and Sean, his partner, used the page to talk about their products shortly before their shipment arrived, and they got a ton of pre-orders from that tactic.

Use engaging content to attract a highly targeted and qualified audience to your online presence, then sell your product to them.

This is an evergreen strategy. You may not use Facebook pages, depending on the nature of your business. But find a social media platform that suits your product. Begin creating content your audience will love even before launching. Also, run social media ads and podcast ads if you have the budget. Some entrepreneurs have also found success from using niche influencers to market their products.

All of these are things you can do without any massive followership. Heck, you can leverage social media even if you just opened accounts today. You just have to be strategic about it.

chapters.title: Success in business requires making your product unique
chapters.text:
Most entrepreneurs think that to be successful in business, you need to reinvent the wheel or change the face of your business entirely, but this is not true.

The key to your product becoming successful is making it different, even if it’s just in one small way. If you keep reinventing or what you have is not unique, you will not get the desired success.

Avoid the trap of constantly innovating; instead, provide what your customers are already seeking.

To make your product successful, focus on refining and creating something unique, not revolutionizing. Striving for perfection is less effective than offering something realistically appealing. Quality and uniqueness do.

Rather than guessing your path to success, prioritize obtaining feedback on your products to make necessary improvements and effectively reach your target audience.

Solve a specific issue with your product, identify and connect with those facing this problem, and use their purchasing responses as valuable feedback.

Pursuing an elusive perfect product or ideal branding keeps your product in a state of fantasy, delaying the financial success you desire.

While some focus on enhancing packaging, redesigning logos, and tweaking colors and fonts, these efforts may not always yield results. What matters is if the product resonates with your customers; they will buy it regardless of the packaging.

Branding, logos, and design play a role in expanding your market presence, but these can evolve over time. Immediate focus should be on avoiding barriers to sales. Product refinement and uniqueness are ongoing tasks, with mistakes being an integral part of the business. Remember, no amount of planning can guarantee a flawless operation.

Did you know? When selling on platforms like Amazon, your packaging often goes unnoticed by customers, as all they see is the standard Amazon delivery box.

chapters.title: Money is a double-edged sword; it can grow your business or kill it
chapters.text:
A good piece of advice for entrepreneurs is to start small and grow fast. You want to bootstrap your business for as long as possible because doing this will give you the independence you need to grow and learn at your own pace.

Aside from making you lose some level of control over the business, taking money from outside investors means you have to be accountable to someone. That can be a good or bad thing, depending on your goal with the business.

If you're after a lifestyle business that will enable you to have the freedom you want, then jumping to get investors' money might not be a good option in your early days. However, if your business has grown to the stage where you need to expand but can't afford the capital, you should seek investors. It will be easier at this stage because you have proof that you can multiply any amount given to you.

Money is an amplifier, not a change agent; it doesn't turn bad ideas into good.

If you need extra money for your business but don't want to get investors involved, there are still two options for you.

First, you could make use of crowdfunding. Sites like Kickstarter have helped entrepreneurs generate the capital they need to expand.

The second option is to get a loan. Debt is often considered a negative thing, but that’s not necessarily so. There's good debt, and there's bad debt. Bad debt is loaning money to spend on liabilities like fancy cars, but when you borrow to invest in a venture that could multiply the money, that's good debt. So, the bottom line is to bootstrap for as long as possible before seeking partnership or taking loans.

chapters.title: Building a customer list
chapters.text:
Choosing a product to sell is a challenging step for many, leading them to opt for a regular 9-to-5 job instead. Determining the nature of your business, identifying your target customers, and producing your product is a significant undertaking. This is the reason why many never reach this phase of business development.

Deciding what to sell is the most important part of the business process.

The old saying holds true: real progress begins with a sale.

In this phase, you're likely to fixate on each detail and agonize over decisions. Temptations to heed varied advice or build intricate sales strategies will arise. However, your primary goal remains: achieve a sale.

Consider compiling a list of potential customers or promoting your products on social media. Engage with these customers directly through calls, emails, or messages. Focusing narrowly at first can spark momentum that rapidly grows. At this stage, your entire focus should be on sales, setting aside grand plans to concentrate on securing orders.

You don't need to be a social media star or have a large following to ensure initial product sales. Smartly tapping into a prospective audience is key.

The early months in business are often termed “The Grind,” characterized by intense effort. It's common for new entrepreneurs to experience discouragement, anxiety, and worry if sales don't materialize immediately. But you can beat this by taking the following positive steps:

• Run advertisements. Your target should be to get more audience for your business.
• Engage that audience with content that appeals to them.
• Document the launch of your product so that your audience is primed and ready to buy when you launch.

Getting to this point is hard work. But if you can do it successfully and get your audience or prospective customers prepared for your product, you will beat any barriers.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
In this summary, we considered steps anyone can take to build a successful company from the ground up. The first thing we dealt with was the entrepreneurial mindset. Not everyone is meant to be an entrepreneur, but the journey is worth it if you're ready to grind and face the risks that come with it.

After deciding to be an entrepreneur, the first thing you have to do is find a product to sell. However, we said that just Googling hot products to sell isn't the way to build a sustainable business.

Start with deciding on a market segment you want to serve. A good strategy is to start with your own life. What's a pain point you wish someone could solve for you? If you've thought hard and can't find any, look at the lives of those around you. How can you make their lives better, easier, or more efficient?

Identifying a market segment and its pain points will give you an idea of the sort of solution to create or improve on. Next, find private label companies or contract manufacturers who can help develop this product. And if it's something you can do yourself, great.

When your product is cooking, start making noise about it on the Internet. Create digital footprints for your brand. Run ads to get more eyeballs on your content, then create pre-order offers that your warmed-up audience can't refuse.

Grow your business from no sales to several sales a day and then to multiple sales per day. As the business grows, you may want to sell it off to bigger brands or keep it. If you decide to sell it, don't make the rookie mistake of not thinking things through and researching enough to negotiate properly. Your business is yours, so you should be in control when negotiating. It's up to you to decide if you want to sell everything or just a stake in the business. Don't let anyone make that decision for you.

Try this

After this summary, start searching for problems you could create products to solve. Once you’ve found something you think your audience will love, conduct research on Amazon and social media to see how profitable similar products are in the niche. Only build a business when the products are selling well.

Read it till the end?
